primary_diagnosis.name: Functional dyspepsia (uninvestigated, postprandial distress syndrome), acutely exacerbated

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic (4 week) postprandial epigastric burning, early satiation, bloating and belching without alarm features is the classic dyspepsia / postprandial distress pattern.
Burning epigastric/retrosternal discomfort and nocturnal symptoms overlap heavily with reflux; empirical acid suppression treats both.
Epigastric pain related to meals can be peptic ulcer; less likely without nocturnal empty-stomach pain, but warrants H. pylori testing and endoscopy if it persists or an alarm feature appears.
Epigastric pain can be anginal equivalent — must be screened for; here it is strictly food-related and reproducible, not exertional, with no radiation, diaphoresis or dyspnoea.
Postprandial upper-abdominal pain can be biliary; screened for by asking about RUQ/right-scapular radiation, fever and jaundice.
All ALARM features negative (no haematemesis, melena, dysphagia, weight loss), symptoms strictly food-related — a benign dyspepsia picture suitable for empirical management.

No exertional trigger, no radiation to arm/jaw, no diaphoresis or dyspnoea; symptoms reproducibly tied to meals — cardiac cause clinically excluded (but remains a safety-net trigger if the character changes).
No RUQ or right-scapular radiation, no fever, no jaundice; central epigastric location and pattern argue against biliary pathology.

differential.diagnosis: functional dyspepsia (postprandial distress syndrome), acutely exacerbated
differential.evidence_basis: Benign uninvestigated dyspepsia; empirical acid suppression + dietary modification + H. pylori testing via GP, with endoscopy reserved for persistence or emergent alarm features.
Remains the key outpatient consideration — H. pylori testing and endoscopy if symptoms persist beyond a PPI trial or an alarm feature develops.

red_flags.description: Gastrointestinal bleeding — haematemesis, coffee-ground vomiting or melena
red_flags.clinical_significance: If present, signals a bleeding peptic ulcer or malignancy and mandates urgent/emergency assessment; its absence here supports safe outpatient management with explicit safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Dysphagia or odynophagia (food sticking / pain on swallowing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An alarm feature raising concern for oesophageal stricture or malignancy requiring prompt endoscopy; absent here.
An alarm feature for upper-GI malignancy; absent here, supporting a benign dyspepsia diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Features of atypical acute coronary syndrome — exertional pain, radiation to arm/jaw, diaphoresis, dyspnoea
red_flags.clinical_significance: Epigastric pain can be an anginal equivalent; screened negative here (strictly food-related, reproducible, no radiation or autonomic features), but remains a mandatory safety-net trigger.

red_flags.description: Peritonism — sudden severe rigid/board-like abdomen, guarding or rebound
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a perforated viscus or acute surgical abdomen; absent here (patient mobile, discomfort mild), but a mandatory safety-net trigger for immediate escalation if it develops.

pathophysiology_summary: Functional dyspepsia is a disorder of gut-brain interaction: visceral hypersensitivity and impaired gastric accommodation/emptying produce postprandial fullness, early satiation and epigastric burning without structural disease. Gastric acid and a hypersensitive, mildly inflamed mucosa amplify the burning discomfort, and gastric distension after large meals triggers the fullness, bloating and belching. Helicobacter pylori infection is a treatable contributor in a subset and should be tested for. Management is empirical acid suppression (a proton pump inhibitor), meal-volume and dietary modification, avoidance of gastric irritants (NSAIDs, smoking, excess caffeine), and 'test-and-treat' for H. pylori — with endoscopy reserved for persistent symptoms or alarm features. The differentials that must be actively excluded (peptic ulcer, biliary disease, atypical cardiac pain) do not require physical examination to screen — a focused history covering alarm features and the exertional/radiation character of the pain is sufficient in telehealth.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swayed by the patient's fear that the pain is 'near the chest' and escalating a benign, food-related dyspepsia to urgent in-person, ED or 000 assessment when the history already excludes cardiac and surgical causes. Over-triaging this case is the central error under test.
Reassuring the patient without first explicitly screening for haematemesis, melena, dysphagia and weight loss — the safety of empirical management depends on that screen being negative.
Suggesting an anti-inflammatory (ibuprofen/naproxen) for the discomfort — NSAIDs erode the gastric mucosal barrier and worsen dyspepsia/ulcer risk. A clear error of commission.
Prescribing H. pylori-eradication triple therapy empirically without a confirmatory breath/stool/biopsy test — testing must precede eradication.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct abdominal palpation — epigastric tenderness, guarding, rebound, hepatosplenomegaly, pulsatile mass
Auscultated bowel sounds and chest examination
Clinician-measured vital signs (BP, HR, temperature, SpO2) without a home device
Helicobacter pylori urea breath test and, if needed, upper GI endoscopy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation is required. The correct telehealth action is to recognise benign uninvestigated dyspepsia, confirm the ALARM-feature screen is negative and the pain is non-cardiac, start an empirical proton pump inhibitor trial, advise smaller more frequent meals and avoidance of NSAIDs/smoking/excess caffeine, provide explicit safety-netting for the alarm features, and arrange a routine GP review within 1-2 weeks to organise H. pylori testing and assess the PPI response.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (classic benign pattern). The real risks are (a) OVER-action — escalating a benign presentation to urgent in-person/ED/000, and (b) failing to safety-net the alarm features so a future GI bleed, dysphagia or cardiac change is not caught.
